\chapter{ITIL Deep Dive}

\index{ITIL Deep Dive}

This part builds on the foundations by examining how mature IT teams use ITIL processes to manage change and drive continual improvement.

\section{Change Enablement vs Release Management}
\index{Change Enablement vs Release Management}
Change enablement and release management are two sides of the same coin. One keeps risky alterations under control, the other moves approved work into production. In practice you need both...

\subsection*{Slide 1: Change Enablement vs Release Management}

\index{Change Enablement vs Release Management}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Change enablement and release management are two sides of the same coin. One keeps risky alterations under control, the other moves approved work into production. Speaker 2: In practice you need both disciplines working together so that updates land smoothly without disrupting users.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Change Enablement vs Release Management

Keeping changes safe while shipping fast

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 2: Change enablement}

\index{Change enablement}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Change enablement focuses on assessing risk before any code or configuration shift happens. Small tweaks might be pre-approved, while major ones get a thorough review. Speaker 2: The goal is to prevent nasty surprises in production. It acts as a safety net so development teams can't accidentally take down critical services.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Change enablement

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Assess risk before work starts
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Require approvals for significant changes
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Protect production stability
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 3: Minor Change Process Flow}

\index{Minor Change Process Flow}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Release management picks up once a change is approved. It bundles related work into versions and schedules them for deployment. Speaker 2: Think of it like publishing a magazine. All the articles need editing before the final issue goes to print, and everyone should know exactly when it's hitting the shelves.

\begin{figure}[htbp]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.86\linewidth]{figures/part-02/change-vs-release/images/itil-minor-change.png}
\caption{ITIL Minor Change Process Flow}
\end{figure}

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Minor Change Process Flow

ITIL Minor Change Process Flow

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 4: Release management}

\index{Release management}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: When change enablement and release management work in harmony, teams can ship quickly without sacrificing stability. Speaker 2: It becomes clear who approves what and when new features will appear, which keeps stakeholders confident that the process is under control.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Release management

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Package changes for deployment
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Coordinate release windows
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Communicate what is shipping
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 5: Working together}

\index{Working together}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Working together

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Change enablement gates the work
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Release management plans the rollout
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Both aim for smooth, reliable updates
\end{itemize}

---

\end{quote}


\section{Building and Maintaining a Configuration Management Database}
\index{Building and Maintaining a Configuration Management Database}
A configuration management database, or CMDB, is the master inventory of systems and services. It lists configuration items and how they depend on each other so support teams have the full...

\subsection*{Slide 1: Building and Maintaining a Configuration Management Database}

\index{Building and Maintaining a Configuration Management Database}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: A configuration management database, or CMDB, is the master inventory of systems and services. Speaker 2: It lists configuration items and how they depend on each other so support teams have the full picture.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Building and Maintaining a Configuration Management Database

Why an accurate inventory powers ITIL

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 2: What is a CMDB?}

\index{What is a CMDB?}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Start by identifying key CIs such as servers, applications and network devices. Speaker 2: Pull in attributes from source systems and link them together to show relationships. Speaker 1: For example, the "web01" server might depend on the "db01" database service.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

What is a CMDB?

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Central inventory of systems and relationships
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Example CIs: servers, network gear, SaaS applications
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Tracks versions, owners and dependencies
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Supports reliable ITIL processes
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 3: Building the CMDB}

\index{Building the CMDB}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Change requests should list the configuration items they will modify. Speaker 2: After approval, update those CI records and compare discovery results to catch any unplanned drift.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Building the CMDB

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Identify key configuration items
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Populate attributes from trusted sources
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Map relationships between CIs
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 4: Change management integration}

\index{Change management integration}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Keeping the CMDB accurate is a continual task. Speaker 2: Automate discovery and update records after every approved change, then audit regularly to find gaps. Speaker 1: Compare the observed state from discovery tools against the approved configuration to detect drift.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Change management integration

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Reference affected CIs in each change request
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Update the CMDB after approved changes
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Link discovery results to spot differences between approved and observed state
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 5: Maintaining accuracy}

\index{Maintaining accuracy}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Sometimes discovery shows a system in a state the CMDB never approved. Speaker 2: This can come from emergency fixes, admins bypassing change control, or forgetting to remove retired equipment. Speaker 1: When observed and authorised states diverge, troubleshooting and audits slow down because teams can't trust the data.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Maintaining accuracy

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Update records as changes happen
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Automate discovery where possible
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Compare observed state to approved CMDB records
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Schedule audits to clean up gaps
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 6: When records and reality differ}

\index{When records and reality differ}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: A well-maintained CMDB accelerates incident troubleshooting and change planning. Speaker 2: It reduces surprises from hidden dependencies and becomes your single source of truth.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

When records and reality differ

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Unauthorized changes bypass change control
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Manual tweaks create configuration drift
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Discovery finds devices not in the CMDB
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Retired assets linger as phantom entries
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 7: Why it matters}

\index{Why it matters}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Why it matters

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Speeds up incident and change analysis
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Reduces risk of unknown dependencies
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Provides a single source of truth
\end{itemize}

---

\end{quote}


\section{Continual Improvement Frameworks and Maturity Assessments}
\index{Continual Improvement Frameworks and Maturity Assessments}
Continual improvement is the heartbeat of any successful IT service organization. It's not a one-time project but an ongoing commitment to making things better every day. This systematic...

\subsection*{Slide 1: Continual Improvement Frameworks and Maturity Assessments}

\index{Continual Improvement Frameworks and Maturity Assessments}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Continual improvement is the heartbeat of any successful IT service organization. It's not a one-time project but an ongoing commitment to making things better every day. Speaker 2: This systematic approach focuses on creating value for customers while learning from every incident, change, and interaction. Think of it as evolving from a reactive firefighting mode to a proactive enhancement culture. Speaker 1: The goal is simple: deliver better service tomorrow than you did today. But achieving this requires both structure and the right mindset across your entire organization.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Continual Improvement Frameworks and Maturity Assessments

Building a culture of ongoing enhancement

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 2: What is continual improvement?}

\index{What is continual improvement?}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: There are two main approaches to improvement: Kaizen and formal improvement programs. Kaizen comes from Japanese manufacturing and means "change for the better." Speaker 2: In Kaizen, everyone makes small daily improvements. A service desk agent might streamline how they document tickets, or a network admin might create a checklist for routine tasks. These small changes add up to significant improvements over time. Speaker 1: Formal improvement programs are bigger structured projects. Think of upgrading your monitoring system or redesigning your change approval process. These need dedicated resources and project management. Speaker 2: [enthusiastically] The magic happens when you combine both approaches. Kaizen keeps the improvement mindset alive day-to-day, while formal programs tackle the bigger transformations your organization needs.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

What is continual improvement?

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Systematic approach to enhancing services
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Focus on value creation and customer satisfaction
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Builds on lessons learned from incidents and changes
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Cultural shift from reactive to proactive mindset
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 3: Kaizen vs. formal improvement programs}

\index{Kaizen vs. formal improvement programs}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: The Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle is your roadmap for systematic improvement. It's like a scientific method for making changes that actually stick. Speaker 2: Plan means identifying what needs improvement and designing a solution. Maybe you've noticed that password reset requests are taking too long, so you plan to implement a self-service portal. Speaker 1: Do is implementing your solution, but start small. Roll out that self-service portal to one department first, not the entire organization. This lets you test and learn without major disruption. Speaker 2: Check means measuring the results. Are password resets faster? Are users happy with the new process? Are there unexpected issues you need to address? Speaker 1: Act is where you decide what to do next. If the pilot worked well, standardize it across the organization. If it didn't, learn from what went wrong and try a different approach. The cycle then begins again.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Kaizen vs. formal improvement programs

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Kaizen: Small, daily improvements by everyone
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Formal programs: Structured projects with defined outcomes
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Both approaches complement each other
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Different time horizons and resource commitments
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 4: The Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle}

\index{The Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Maturity models are like a GPS for your improvement journey. They help you figure out where you are now and chart a path to where you want to be. Speaker 2: Think of it like learning to drive. You start with basic skills like steering and braking, then progress to parallel parking, and eventually to driving in complex traffic. Each level builds on the previous one. Speaker 1: In IT service management, maturity models assess how well your organization manages services. A level one organization might be reactive, fixing things as they break. A level five organization predicts and prevents problems before they impact users. Speaker 2: The key insight is that you can't skip levels. You need solid incident management before you can do effective problem management. You need good change control before you can implement continuous deployment. It's about building capability progressively.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

The Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Plan: Identify improvement opportunities
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Do: Implement changes on a small scale
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Check: Measure results and gather feedback
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Act: Standardize successful improvements
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 5: Maturity models overview}

\index{Maturity models overview}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: ITIL 4 maturity assessment looks at four key dimensions. Think of them as the four legs of a table - you need all of them to be strong for the table to be stable. Speaker 2: Capabilities are what your organization can do. Can you resolve incidents quickly? Can you implement changes without breaking things? Can you plan capacity to meet future demand? Speaker 1: Practices are how work gets done. Are your processes documented and followed? Do you have standard operating procedures? Are your workflows efficient and effective? Speaker 2: Governance covers decision-making and oversight. Who has authority to approve changes? How are resources allocated? How is risk managed? Is there clear accountability? Speaker 1: Culture is about values and behaviors. Do people share knowledge freely? Is there a blame-free environment for learning from mistakes? Are teams collaborative or siloed? Culture often determines whether your improvements will succeed or fail.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Maturity models overview

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Assess current capability levels
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Identify gaps and improvement priorities
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Provide roadmap for progression
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Enable benchmarking against industry standards
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 6: ITIL 4 maturity dimensions}

\index{ITIL 4 maturity dimensions}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: You can't improve what you don't measure. But measuring improvement success goes beyond just technical metrics - you need to look at the whole picture. Speaker 2: Service performance metrics are the obvious starting point. Are your incidents being resolved faster? Are there fewer outages? Is system availability improving? These operational metrics show if your processes are working better. Speaker 1: Customer satisfaction scores tell you if your improvements actually matter to users. Sometimes technical improvements don't translate to better user experience, and sometimes small changes make a huge difference to customer happiness. Speaker 2: Employee engagement levels are crucial but often overlooked. Are your team members more motivated? Do they feel empowered to make improvements? Happy employees deliver better service, so this is a leading indicator of future success. Speaker 1: Finally, business value delivered is the ultimate measure. Are your improvements helping the organization achieve its goals? Are IT services enabling new business capabilities? This connects your technical work to real business outcomes.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

ITIL 4 maturity dimensions

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Capabilities: What the organization can do
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Practices: How work gets done
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Governance: Decision-making and oversight
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Culture: Values and behaviors
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 7: Measuring improvement success}

\index{Measuring improvement success}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Measuring improvement success

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Service performance metrics
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Customer satisfaction scores
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Employee engagement levels
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Business value delivered
\end{itemize}

---

\end{quote}


\section{Metrics \& Reporting Dashboards}
\index{Metrics \& Reporting Dashboards}
Dashboards are the way we turn thousands of ticket updates and log records into a single page the CIO can understand at a glance. When designed well, those tiny coloured boxes and trend...

\subsection*{Slide 1: Metrics \& Reporting Dashboards}

\index{Metrics \& Reporting Dashboards}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Dashboards are the way we turn thousands of ticket updates and log records into a single page the CIO can understand at a glance. Speaker 2: When designed well, those tiny coloured boxes and trend lines become an early-warning system that saves you from unpleasant surprise incidents.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Metrics \& Reporting Dashboards

Turning raw data into actionable insight

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 2: Why metrics matter}

\index{Why metrics matter}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Metrics act like a health check for each ITIL practice. If incident backlogs spike, the numbers will show it long before end-users start shouting. Speaker 2: They also give us proof when things are improving. A downward trend in mean-time-to-restore beats anecdotal "I think we're faster now" any day.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Why metrics matter

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Validate that processes are working as designed
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Spot bottlenecks before they become incidents
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Provide evidence for continual-improvement discussions
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 3: Choosing the right KPIs}

\index{Choosing the right KPIs}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: When picking KPIs, start with what the customer actually cares about—availability, response time, resolution quality. Speaker 2: Mix in leading indicators like change success rate so you can act before outages happen, but avoid the fifty-metric dashboard that no one reads.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Choosing the right KPIs

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Align with customer value rather than gut feel
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Balance leading and lagging indicators
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Keep the list short – focus drives behaviour
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 4: Building the dashboard}

\index{Building the dashboard}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Start with a single pane of glass that pulls ticket data from ServiceNow, uptime from your monitoring stack and config context from the CMDB. Speaker 2: Then layer on traffic-light widgets—green for on-target, amber for at-risk, red for breach—so any stakeholder can see the story in three seconds.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Building the dashboard

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Combine data from ITSM, monitoring and CMDB sources
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Use traffic-light visuals for quick scanning
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Let stakeholders drill down for root-cause context
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 5: Storytelling with data}

\index{Storytelling with data}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Numbers alone rarely move people. Pair every chart with a one-sentence takeaway—“We cut P1 resolution time by 30 \% last quarter.” Speaker 2: That narrative makes the data memorable and, more importantly, sparks the discussions that keep the improvement loop turning.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Storytelling with data

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Frame trends in plain language, not just charts
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Celebrate wins and highlight risks
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Close the loop: feed insights back into the service value chain
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 6: From reports to action}

\index{From reports to action}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Dashboards only matter if they trigger action. Build a cadence—daily stand-ups, weekly ops reviews—where owners commit to fixes on the spot. Speaker 2: Track those tasks in the same tool so next week’s dashboard shows whether the needle actually moved. That’s when metrics become culture.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

From reports to action

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Schedule regular review rituals (daily stand-up, weekly ops review)
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Tie improvement tasks to owners and due dates
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Track progress to prove the dashboard drives change
\end{itemize}

---

\end{quote}


\section{Problem Management Techniques for Root Cause Analysis}
\index{Problem Management Techniques for Root Cause Analysis}
Problem management digs into the why behind repeat incidents so we can stop firefighting the same issues over and over. It's about stepping back, analysing patterns and addressing the real...

\subsection*{Slide 1: Problem Management Techniques for Root Cause Analysis}

\index{Problem Management Techniques for Root Cause Analysis}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Problem management digs into the why behind repeat incidents so we can stop firefighting the same issues over and over. Speaker 2: It's about stepping back, analysing patterns and addressing the real root cause rather than just clearing another ticket.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Problem Management Techniques for Root Cause Analysis

Solving recurring issues at the source

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 2: When to use problem management}

\index{When to use problem management}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: We invoke problem management when incidents keep cropping up or the impact is too big to ignore. Speaker 2: Think chronic outages or situations where quick fixes won't cut it—we need a deeper look to stop the bleeding.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

When to use problem management

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Repeated incidents pointing to a common cause
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item High impact issues without an obvious fix
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Need to prevent further disruptions
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 3: Root cause analysis methods}

\index{Root cause analysis methods}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Techniques like the 5 Whys or fishbone diagrams help track symptoms back to the true cause. Speaker 2: By linking related incidents, we can see patterns and gather evidence from every team involved.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Root cause analysis methods

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item 5 Whys and fishbone diagrams
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Link incident records to underlying problems
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Collaborate across teams to gather evidence
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 4: Embedding the lessons}

\index{Embedding the lessons}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Once we've nailed the root cause, document the workaround and roll out changes to eliminate it. Speaker 2: Review whether those fixes stick. Problem management is a cycle of learning and preventing future pain.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Embedding the lessons

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Document workarounds and known errors
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Implement changes to remove the cause
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Review effectiveness and refine processes
\end{itemize}

---

\end{quote}


\section{Service Level Agreements, OLAs and KPIs}
\index{Service Level Agreements, OLAs and KPIs}
Service level agreements, or SLAs, spell out exactly what customers can expect from the support team. They keep everyone honest about response times and resolution goals. Think of them as...

\subsection*{Slide 1: Service Level Agreements, OLAs and KPIs}

\index{Service Level Agreements, OLAs and KPIs}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Service level agreements, or SLAs, spell out exactly what customers can expect from the support team. They keep everyone honest about response times and resolution goals. Speaker 2: Think of them as the ground rules for collaboration. If an SLA gets breached, it usually triggers extra scrutiny or penalties, so they're worth taking seriously.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Service Level Agreements, OLAs and KPIs

Defining success for support teams

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 2: Understanding SLAs}

\index{Understanding SLAs}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: A solid SLA defines the services covered, the hours of support and how quickly the team needs to respond or resolve different issue types. Speaker 2: It also spells out the consequences if those targets aren't met. No one likes a penalty clause, but it's there to keep priorities clear when systems break.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Understanding SLAs

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Set expectations with customers
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Document response and resolution times
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Outline consequences for missed targets
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 3: Operational Level Agreements}

\index{Operational Level Agreements}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Operational level agreements, or OLAs, sit behind the scenes. They define how internal teams support each other so that customer-facing SLAs can be met. Speaker 2: Imagine the database team promising the app team a five-minute failover. Without that kind of OLA, it would be hard to guarantee the bigger service commitments.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Operational Level Agreements

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Internal commitments between teams
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Clarify dependencies for each workflow
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Keep cross-functional support on track
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 4: KPIs and continual improvement}

\index{KPIs and continual improvement}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Key performance indicators turn these agreements into measurable results. Response time, first-call resolution and uptime are common examples. Speaker 2: Tracking KPIs month over month shows whether your processes actually work. If a target keeps slipping, it's a prompt to refine the workflow or renegotiate the SLA.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

KPIs and continual improvement

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Measure service health and trends
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Report progress to stakeholders
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Adjust targets as business needs change
\end{itemize}

---

\end{quote}
